one_line_takeaway: The paper defines and operationalizes a new problem -- prompt-level, memory-level 'unlearning' for LLM-driven agents (as opposed to weight-level LLM unlearning) -- and shows a DPO-trained request-to-prompt conversion model beats two self-constructed prompting baselines at both erasing targeted agent behavior and resisting a black-box behavioral-inference/reconstruction adversary, across GridWorld, AlfWorld, HotPotQA, and HumanEval with GPT-4o-Mini, Claude-3-Haiku, and Llama-3.3-70B.
key_finding: Across GridWorld, the NL-based method achieves high Unlearn@1 (e.g., state unlearning: 0.97-0.99 across GPT/Claude/Llama; trajectory: 0.97-0.98; environment: 1.00) while task success rate on other tasks stays at 1.0, versus Code-based (0.87-0.91 Unlearn@1) and Example-based (0.73-0.81), the latter degrading further in AlfWorld (e.g., state unlearning Example-based (GPT) success rate collapses from 0.20 before to 0.13 after unlearning, vs. NL-based (GPT) 0.95->0.93). In the inference-attack evaluation (Table XXVIII-XXX, GridWorld), NL-based unlearning reduces the probability an unlearned agent revisits a forgotten state from ~0.97-0.99 (pre-unlearn) down to 0.01-0.02, statistically indistinguishable from an agent that never saw the state (0.01-0.02); for environment reconstruction, NL-based drops the adversary's successful-reconstruction rate from ~93-98% (pre-unlearn) to ~12-18%, close to the never-seen baseline (~13-17%), whereas Code-based (28-35%) and Example-based (40-45%) leave substantially more residual information recoverable. Ablations show the conversion model C is necessary (e.g., GridWorld state unlearning Unlearn@1 drops from 0.95 to 0.63 for GPT without C) and fine-tuning it matters (0.95 -> 0.82 without fine-tuning), and m=3 generated prompts per request is a sweet spot (m=1 much worse, m=5 only marginal gain over m=3). The paper also reports a specific model-behavior asymmetry: Claude-3-Haiku consistently shows weaker unlearning capability/lower Unlearn@1 than GPT-4o-Mini and Llama-3.3-70B (e.g., HotPotQA state unlearning Unlearn@1: Claude=0, GPT=1, Llama=1 across NL/Code/Example variants), attributed by the authors to Claude failing to infer implicit task semantics (e.g., not recognizing that placing a 'hot' mug in a cabinet implies a heating action that should have been forgotten), while GPT-4o-Mini sometimes over-generalizes an unlearning instruction (unlearning heating-related tasks also erroneously suppresses non-heating microwave-placement tasks).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'LLM-based agent unlearning' as a new research problem distinct from both LLM (weight-level) unlearning and reinforcement unlearning, since an agent's behavior is governed by a frozen LLM's policy via prompts/memory rather than trainable parameters. It (1) proposes a framework categorizing unlearning requests into three scenarios -- state unlearning (forgetting specific states/items within a trajectory), trajectory unlearning (forgetting a specific sequence of state-action pairs while still allowing individual states to be visited), and environment unlearning (forgetting an entire environment/task category, causing the agent to behave as if encountering it for the first time); (2) proposes a 'NL-based' method that trains a conversion model C (initialized from a base LM such as GPT-Neo-2.7B) via a DPO-style preference loss (Eq. 10) to translate a natural-language unlearning request x into an actionable unlearning prompt y that is fed to the target LLM to steer agent behavior, with preferred/dispreferred prompt pairs generated by testing whether the resulting agent behavior satisfies the unlearning objective; (3) supplies theoretical analysis (utility/Bradley-Terry derivation, linear convergence proof under smoothness/strong-convexity assumptions, and a KL-divergence bound between the unlearned agent's behavior distribution and a reference agent's) arguing the method converges and produces behaviorally indistinguishable-from-naive agents; and (4) designs an 'unlearning inference adversary' that queries the agent and observes its behavior (rather than using prompt injection) to try to detect or reconstruct the forgotten state/trajectory/environment, used to empirically evaluate robustness of the unlearning.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model (Section III and Section VIII) is privacy/security-motivated but behavioral, not a classic exploit threat model: the agent owner controls the LLM-based agent, can query the LLM and observe outputs, but cannot access or modify the underlying LLM's internal parameters (unlearning is triggered by a data-erasure-style request, e.g. a property owner invoking a 'right to erasure', or an area being reclassified as security-restricted). The adversary of interest (Section VIII, 'Privacy Threats to Our Framework') is an unlearning-inference adversary: it interacts with the deployed agent purely through natural-language task instructions and behavioral observation (no prompt injection, no parameter access), and tries to (a) determine whether a suspected state/trajectory was unlearned by constructing tasks whose optimal solution requires visiting/traversing it and checking if the agent takes suboptimal detours, or (b) reconstruct an entire unlearned environment's spatial layout from the agent's exploratory state-action trajectories using an existing reconstruction technique (cited, not the paper's own). The paper explicitly notes that standard prompt-injection attacks (cited work) do not apply in this setting because the adversary does not know a priori what was unlearned and has no predefined steering target.
limitations: The paper is explicit that this is the first work on LLM-based agent unlearning, so its two baselines (Code-based, Example-based) are the authors' own adaptations of unrelated prior prompting techniques rather than competing unlearning methods -- there is no external unlearning baseline for direct comparison. Unlearning execution relies on explicit rule-based 'memory removal + action inhibition' steps layered on top of the learned conversion prompt (Section IV, 'Unlearning Execution'), and the paper does not evaluate what happens if the base LLM's own future training/fine-tuning re-exposes it to the same data (i.e., unlearning is enforced at the prompt/memory layer per-agent-instance, not at the model-weight level, so it is explicitly not resistant to anyone with access to the raw LLM weights or a differently configured agent instance built on the same LLM). The theoretical convergence/KL bounds (Section V) rely on idealized assumptions (bounded feature norm ||Δψ|| ≤ B, strongly-convex feature covariance, Bradley-Terry preference generation) that are not empirically verified against the actual trained conversion model. The scalability study (varying number of unlearning states/trajectories/environments) is deferred entirely to the Appendix and not included in the extracted main-body text reviewed here. The inference-adversary evaluation is conducted only in the custom GridWorld environment (chosen 'for its interpretability'), not in AlfWorld, HotPotQA, or HumanEval, so robustness-to-inference claims are not validated across all four evaluation platforms. The adversary model explicitly excludes prompt injection and any access to LLM internals/logits, so robustness claims apply only to the specific black-box behavioral-inference adversary the authors constructed.

datasets_used: GridWorld (custom 2D grid environment built by the authors)
AlfWorld (134 tasks across 6 categories: put, puttwo, clean, heat, cool, examine)
Gazebo-simulated household environment with a mobile robot (qualitative robustness check, no quantitative table)
quantitative_results: GridWorld state unlearning Unlearn@1: NL-based 0.97-0.99, Code-based 0.87-0.88, Example-based 0.73-0.79 (across GPT/Claude/Llama)
GridWorld environment unlearning: all methods reach Unlearn efficacy = 1.0 and Unlearn@1 = 1.0; steps-to-complete in target environment rise from ~20-22 (before) to ~48-61 (after) for NL/Code/Example
AlfWorld state unlearning: Example-based (GPT) success rate collapses from 0.20 (before) to 0.13 (after) vs NL-based (GPT) 0.95 -> 0.93
HotPotQA state unlearning Unlearn@1: Claude=0 for both NL-based and Code-based, vs GPT=1 and Llama=1
Inference attack (GridWorld, NL-based, GPT): probability of traversing unlearned state drops from 0.99 (pre-unlearn) to 0.02 (unlearned), vs 0.01 for an agent that never saw the state

Environment reconstruction attack (GridWorld, NL-based): successful reconstruction rate drops from ~96% (pre-unlearn) to ~12-18% (post-unlearn), vs ~13-17% for a never-exposed agent; Code-based leaves 28-35% and Example-based 33-45% reconstructable
Ablation, remove conversion model C (GridWorld state unlearning, GPT): Unlearn@1 drops from 0.95 (with C) to 0.63 (without C)
Ablation, remove fine-tuning of C (GridWorld state unlearning, GPT): Unlearn efficacy drops from 0.99 to 0.88
Ablation, number of generated prompts m (GridWorld state unlearning, GPT): Unlearn efficacy 0.72 (m=1) vs 0.98 (m=3) vs 0.99 (m=5)
baselines_compared: Code-based prompting (authors' own adaptation, inspired by the code-conversion idea in AdaPlanner, cited work [29])
Example-based prompting (authors' own adaptation, inspired by the exemplar-prompting idea in SYNAPSE, cited work [30])
Unfine-tuned / no-conversion-model ablations of the paper's own NL-based method (internal ablation baselines, not external prior work)
